learn.title: Solar Panel Types
learn.intro: Solar panels are crucial for harnessing sunlight efficiently. Monocrystalline panels are highly efficient but costly, polycrystalline offer a balance, and thin-film is flexible but less efficient. Choosing the right type impacts cost, space, and energy output, making them key to scalable renewable solutions.
learn.c1_text: Monocrystalline panels use single-crystal silicon, achieving 15-22% efficiency. They’re ideal for limited spaces, like rooftops, where maximizing output per square meter is critical. For example, a 1 kW monocrystalline system needs ~4.5 sq m, compared to 5.5 sq m for polycrystalline.
learn.c2_text: Polycrystalline panels, made from multiple silicon fragments, cost 10-15% less than monocrystalline. With 13-16% efficiency, they’re popular in India for large-scale installations where budget matters more than space, such as community solar farms.
learn.c3_text: Thin-film panels, using materials like cadmium telluride, are lightweight and flexible. They perform better in low light but require more space. India’s arid regions use them for large solar parks, though their 10-12% efficiency means larger installations are needed.
learn.stat1: India installed 90 GW solar capacity in 2024, with monocrystalline dominating rooftop markets.
learn.stat2: Monocrystalline panels cost ~₹50-70/W, while thin-film is ~₹30-40/W.
learn.stat3: Thin-film installations require 20-30% more area than monocrystalline for same output.
learn.stat4: Polycrystalline remains 20% cheaper than monocrystalline in India’s utility-scale projects.

learn.title: Photoelectric Effect in Solar
learn.intro: The photoelectric effect is how solar panels convert sunlight into electricity. When photons hit semiconductor materials, they knock electrons loose, creating current. This principle, discovered by Einstein, powers all modern PV systems, making it foundational to solar energy.
learn.c1_text: Solar cells act like diodes. When sunlight hits the semiconductor (e.g., silicon), photons dislodge electrons, creating a flow. A 1 m² panel with 20% efficiency can generate ~250 watts under full sun, equivalent to charging a smartphone every 2 minutes.
learn.c2_text: Efficiency drops as temperature rises. For every 1°C increase, output may fall 0.3-0.5%. In India’s hot climates, panels may lose 10-15% efficiency during peak heat, highlighting the need for ventilation or cooling systems.
learn.c3_text: India’s solar parks use tracking systems to maximize photon capture. During monsoons, reduced sunlight lowers output, but systems still generate 60-70% of annual energy, proving reliability in variable weather.
learn.stat1: A 1 kW solar system in India produces ~1,500 kWh/year, enough to power a household for 15 months.
learn.stat2: Silicon solar cells have ~20% efficiency under standard test conditions.
learn.stat3: Temperature coefficient for most panels is -0.3% to -0.5% per °C.
learn.stat4: Tracking systems can boost annual output by 25-30% in India.

learn.title: Rooftop Solar in India
learn.intro: Rooftop solar empowers households to generate clean energy, reducing grid reliance. In India, net metering allows excess power to be fed back, lowering bills. This policy drives urban adoption, making solar accessible and economically viable for millions.
learn.c1_text: A 3 kW rooftop system in Delhi can meet 70% of a family’s electricity needs. With net metering, surplus power credits offset bills. For example, a household might pay ₹1,000/month for grid power but earn ₹1,500 in credits, netting savings.
learn.c2_text: Net metering lets users sell excess power to the grid at retail rates. In Tamil Nadu, a 5 kW system can generate ₹15,000/year in credits. However, policy variations exist—some states cap credits or require upfront payments.
learn.c3_text: India’s MNRE targets 40 GW rooftop solar by 2026. Cities like Jaipur have 10,000+ installations. Subsidies cover 40-60% of costs, making rooftop solar affordable for middle-class families and boosting rural electrification.
learn.stat1: India has 12 GW rooftop solar capacity as of 2024, with 60% in urban areas.
learn.stat2: Net metering tariffs in Maharashtra are ₹6-8/kWh for exported power.
learn.stat3: Subsidies reduce rooftop system costs by ₹15,000-25,000 per kW.
learn.stat4: Jaipur’s rooftop solar covers 8% of the city’s total electricity demand.

learn.title: Building Integrated PV
learn.intro: BIPV merges solar technology with architecture, turning buildings into energy generators. Instead of rooftop panels, solar cells are embedded in windows, facades, or tiles. This approach enhances aesthetics while generating power, ideal for urban spaces where land is scarce.
learn.c1_text: BIPV uses materials like solar glass or tiles. For example, a building’s facade with BIPV can generate 15-20% of its energy needs. The Burj Khalifa in Dubai uses BIPV in its exterior, producing enough power for internal lighting.
learn.c2_text: Unlike bulky panels, BIPV blends with design. Solar tiles mimic traditional roofing, while transparent panels in windows allow light. In India, a Mumbai office complex uses BIPV glass, reducing energy costs by 30% without compromising aesthetics.
learn.c3_text: India’s BIPV market is nascent but growing. The government’s push for green buildings includes BIPV incentives. A Delhi school installed BIPV panels in its roof and walls, cutting electricity bills by ₹50,000 annually and meeting 40% of its energy needs.
learn.stat1: BIPV can reduce building energy costs by 15-30% in optimal conditions.
learn.stat2: India’s green building policy mandates BIPV in 20% of new commercial projects by 2025.
learn.stat3: Solar glass costs ~₹1,200/sq m, 20% higher than standard glass but with energy payback.
learn.stat4: BIPV adoption in India is 5% of total solar installations, expected to rise to 15% by 2030.

learn.title: Microgrids for Resilience
learn.intro: Microgrids are localized energy systems that operate independently or with the main grid. They enhance resilience by providing power during outages, especially in remote areas. In villages and disaster-prone regions, microgrids ensure continuous energy access, supporting critical services and economic activities.
learn.c1_text: A microgrid in rural India uses solar and batteries to power 500 homes. During monsoon floods, when the grid fails, the microgrid keeps hospitals and schools running. It’s like a self-contained city grid, independent of external power lines.
learn.c2_text: Microgrids store excess energy in batteries. For example, a 10 kW microgrid with a 20 kWh battery can supply power for 2 hours during outages. This is critical for disaster resilience, ensuring backup for emergency services and communication networks.
learn.c3_text: After the 2023 Kerala floods, a microgrid-powered clinic operated for 72 hours without grid power. In India, MNRE funds microgrids in flood-prone areas, combining renewables with storage to protect communities from climate risks.
learn.stat1: India has 1,200 microgrids, powering 2 million people in remote areas.
learn.stat2: A 5 kW microgrid costs ~₹3-4 lakh, including solar, batteries, and inverters.
learn.stat3: Microgrids reduce diesel generator use by 70% in rural India.
learn.stat4: Post-disaster, microgrids restore power 30% faster than grid recovery.

learn.title: Electric Commercial Vehicles
learn.intro: Electric buses and trucks are transforming commercial transport by cutting emissions and operating costs. Powered by batteries or hydrogen, they offer quieter, cleaner alternatives to diesel. Governments worldwide are incentivizing their adoption to meet climate goals and reduce urban pollution.
learn.c1_text: A single electric bus can replace 50 diesel vehicles in emissions. For example, Mumbai’s electric bus fleet saves 1,200 tons of CO2 annually. With lower maintenance and fuel costs, they’re 30% cheaper to operate over 5 years compared to diesel buses.
learn.c2_text: Modern EV batteries have 250-300 km range per charge. A 400 kWh battery powers a truck for 300 km, equivalent to 150 liters of diesel. Fast-charging stations can recharge in 30 minutes, making them viable for urban delivery routes.
learn.c3_text: India’s FAME II scheme subsidizes 40% of electric bus costs. Delhi has 150 electric buses, reducing particulate pollution by 40% in key corridors. Trucks like Tata’s Nexon EV are used for last-mile deliveries, cutting logistics costs by 20%.
learn.stat1: India plans 10,000 electric buses by 2025 under FAME II.
learn.stat2: Electric trucks save ~₹10,000/month in fuel costs per vehicle.
learn.stat3: Battery prices have dropped 70% since 2010, making EVs cost-competitive.
learn.stat4: Tata Motors launched 500 electric commercial vehicles in 2023.
